title: Food Allergy
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Allergy/Immunology Fellows, Primary Care
objectives: Distinguish IgE-mediated from non-IgE-mediated food allergy
Interpret skin prick testing and specific IgE in diagnosis
Understand the role of oral food challenge as gold standard
Apply management principles: avoidance, emergency action plans, epinephrine
Discuss emerging treatments: oral immunotherapy and omalizumab

guidelineReferences.name: NIAID Food Allergy Guidelines
guidelineReferences.citation: Boyce JA, et al. Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of food allergy in the United States. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2010;126:S1-S58
keyPoints: Oral food challenge is gold standard for diagnosis
SPT and sIgE identify sensitization, not clinical allergy

guidelineReferences.name: EAACI IgE-mediated Food Allergy Guidelines 2024
guidelineReferences.citation: Santos AF, et al. EAACI guidelines on the management of IgE-mediated food allergy. Allergy. 2025;80:14-36

vignette.text:
A 3-year-old boy is brought to your clinic after an episode of hives and facial swelling 30 minutes after eating peanut butter for the first time yesterday. His mother reports he developed widespread hives, lip swelling, and vomited once. She gave diphenhydramine and symptoms resolved over 2 hours. He did not have difficulty breathing or become lethargic.

He has a history of eczema and has been drinking cow's milk formula without issue. He tolerates eggs in baked goods but has never had scrambled eggs.

Past Medical History: Atopic dermatitis (moderate), no prior food reactions

Family History: Father has seasonal allergies, older sister has peanut allergy

Medications: Hydrocortisone 1% cream PRN for eczema

physicalExam.skin: Dry skin with flexural eczema, no acute urticaria, no angioedema today
physicalExam.HEENT: No lip or facial swelling, oropharynx clear

questions.stem: This child had an immediate reaction to peanut with urticaria, angioedema, and vomiting.
questions.question: How are food allergic reactions classified?

options.text: Food intolerance and allergy are the same

IgE-mediated: Immediate onset (minutes to 2 hours), urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis, respiratory symptoms; Non-IgE-mediated: Delayed onset (hours to days), GI-predominant (FPIES, proctocolitis, EoE); Mixed: Features of both (eczema exacerbation)
All food allergies are IgE-mediated
Delayed reactions don't exist

questions.explanation: Food allergy classification: (1) IgE-MEDIATED: Immediate (minutes-2 hours), mast cell/basophil activation, symptoms include urticaria, angioedema, vomiting, respiratory symptoms, anaphylaxis; (2) NON-IgE-MEDIATED: Delayed (hours-days), T-cell mediated, includes FPIES (vomiting 2-4 hours post-ingestion), allergic proctocolitis (bloody stools in infants), eosinophilic GI diseases; (3) MIXED IgE/non-IgE: Atopic dermatitis, eosinophilic esophagitis. This child's reaction (30 min onset, urticaria, angioedema, vomiting) is classic IgE-mediated.

whyChain.answer: IgE-mediated reactions occur within minutes because preformed IgE on mast cells immediately triggers degranulation. Non-IgE reactions take hours to days because they require T-cell activation. This affects diagnosis (IgE testing only detects IgE-mediated allergy) and management (epinephrine for IgE-mediated anaphylaxis).

whyChain.why: What is food intolerance vs allergy?
whyChain.answer: Food INTOLERANCE is non-immune (e.g., lactose intolerance from enzyme deficiency). Food ALLERGY is immune-mediated. 1 in 3 people report food allergy but only ~1 in 20 have true allergy when tested. Self-reported allergy often overestimates true prevalence.

questions.stem: You are interpreting this child's allergy testing results.
questions.question: What is the diagnostic approach for IgE-mediated food allergy?
questions.hint: Consider the role of history, testing, and food challenge.

options.text: Total IgE diagnoses food allergy
sIgE testing is unnecessary if SPT is positive

History + IgE sensitization (SPT or sIgE) together support diagnosis; SPT/sIgE alone show SENSITIZATION, not clinical ALLERGY; Oral food challenge is the GOLD STANDARD for diagnosis; Component testing (e.g., Ara h 2) improves specificity
Positive skin test alone confirms food allergy

questions.explanation: Diagnostic approach: (1) HISTORY: Detailed reaction history (timing, symptoms, reproducibility) is essential first step; (2) IgE TESTING: Skin prick test (SPT) or specific IgE (sIgE) detect SENSITIZATION - presence of IgE does NOT equal clinical allergy (many sensitized patients tolerate the food); (3) COMPONENT TESTING: sIgE to specific allergenic proteins (Ara h 2 for peanut, Cor a 14 for hazelnut) improves diagnostic accuracy; (4) ORAL FOOD CHALLENGE (OFC): Gold standard - controlled ingestion under medical supervision. This child has convincing history + high sIgE/Ara h 2 - peanut allergy is confirmed without needing challenge.

whyChain.why: Why can't positive testing alone diagnose allergy?
whyChain.answer: Sensitization (positive SPT/sIgE) can exist without clinical reactivity - the patient tolerates the food despite having IgE. This is especially common with cross-reactive allergens. Only ~50% of sensitized individuals are clinically allergic. Diagnosis requires history + sensitization, or oral challenge.

Ara h 2 is a 2S albumin storage protein in peanut - a 'major allergen' that's heat-stable and associated with systemic reactions. High Ara h 2 (>1-2 kU/L) has >90% PPV for clinical peanut allergy. Much better than total peanut sIgE for predicting true allergy, especially in pollen-sensitized patients.

questions.stem: He has positive testing to cashew but has never eaten it.
questions.question: What is the significance of the positive cashew testing without prior exposure?

options.text: Tree nuts never cross-react
He is definitely allergic to all tree nuts

Tree nut cross-sensitization is common; cashew/pistachio cluster together; walnut/pecan cluster together; positive test without reaction history = sensitization, not confirmed allergy - may need oral challenge to determine if truly allergic or just cross-sensitized
Positive testing is always a false positive

questions.explanation: Cross-sensitization vs cross-reactivity: Positive testing to a food never eaten indicates cross-sensitization (shared IgE epitopes). Tree nut cross-reactivity patterns: Cashew-pistachio (highly cross-reactive, ~70%), Walnut-pecan (highly cross-reactive), Almond often cross-reacts with stone fruits. Peanut (legume) and tree nuts are botanically unrelated but clinical co-allergy is common (~30%). This child is sensitized to cashew - but may or may not react clinically. Options: strict avoidance OR oral food challenge to clarify.

whyChain.why: Should he avoid all tree nuts?
whyChain.answer: Conservative approach: Avoid all tree nuts given peanut allergy + tree nut sensitization (often recommended for young children). Alternative: Consider supervised oral food challenge to nuts with lower sIgE (almond) if family wants to expand diet. Cashew (high sIgE 15 kU/L) should be avoided. Individual decisions based on risk tolerance.

questions.stem: You are counseling the family on managing this child's peanut allergy.
questions.question: What are the key components of food allergy management?
questions.hint: Consider avoidance, emergency preparedness, and education.

options.text: Complete avoidance is unnecessary
Antihistamines alone are sufficient treatment

Strict allergen AVOIDANCE (read labels, avoid cross-contamination), prescribe EPINEPHRINE auto-injector (first-line for anaphylaxis), written EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN, education on symptom recognition, medical alert identification, dietitian referral if needed
Epinephrine is a last resort

questions.explanation: Food allergy management pillars: (1) AVOIDANCE: Read all food labels, aware of cross-contamination (shared equipment), FALCPA requires labeling of top 8 allergens (milk, egg, peanut, tree nuts, soy, wheat, fish, shellfish + sesame now added); (2) EPINEPHRINE: Auto-injector (EpiPen, Auvi-Q) is FIRST-LINE for anaphylaxis - give immediately for systemic symptoms, prescribe 2 devices; (3) EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN: Written instructions for caregivers, schools; (4) EDUCATION: Recognize symptoms, when to give epinephrine, when to call 911; (5) Medical alert bracelet; (6) Dietitian for nutritional guidance.

whyChain.answer: Antihistamines only treat cutaneous symptoms and work slowly. Epinephrine reverses ALL anaphylaxis features: bronchospasm (β2), hypotension (α1), and inhibits further mediator release. Delayed epinephrine is associated with fatal anaphylaxis. Antihistamines are adjunctive, not first-line.

Give epinephrine for: any respiratory symptoms (wheeze, stridor, cough), cardiovascular symptoms (hypotension, pale/floppy), severe GI symptoms with other system involvement, progressive symptoms, prior severe reactions. When in doubt, give it - risk of withholding far exceeds risk of giving.

questions.stem: A different patient: 25-year-old with seasonal allergies reports mouth itching when eating raw apples and carrots.
questions.question: What is oral allergy syndrome (pollen-food allergy syndrome)?

options.text: OAS is unrelated to pollen allergy

OAS: IgE cross-reactivity between pollen and raw fruits/vegetables with homologous proteins; symptoms LOCALIZED to mouth (itching, tingling, mild swelling); heat-labile proteins - COOKED foods usually tolerated; rarely causes systemic reactions
Cooked foods cause the same reaction
OAS causes severe anaphylaxis

questions.explanation: Oral Allergy Syndrome (Pollen-Food Allergy Syndrome): (1) Mechanism: IgE cross-reactivity between pollen proteins and homologous proteins in raw fruits/vegetables; (2) Common patterns: Birch pollen → apple, pear, cherry, carrot, hazelnut, soy; Ragweed → melon, banana; Grass → tomato, melon; (3) Symptoms: Localized to oropharynx (itching, tingling, mild lip/tongue swelling), resolves quickly; (4) Heat-labile: Cooking denatures proteins → cooked forms usually tolerated; (5) Rarely systemic but ~2-9% may have more severe reactions. SPT to fresh food often positive when commercial extract negative.

whyChain.answer: Most OAS patients do NOT need epinephrine prescription - reactions stay localized. However, consider epinephrine if: prior systemic reaction, reaction to cooked form, reaction to highly allergenic foods (soy, hazelnut), or significant symptoms beyond oropharynx.

sources.text: StatPearls: Food Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The family asks about treatments beyond avoidance for peanut allergy.
questions.question: What are the emerging treatment options for food allergy?

options.text: ORAL IMMUNOTHERAPY (OIT): Gradual increasing doses to raise reaction threshold (desensitization), approved Palforzia for peanut ages 4-17; OMALIZUMAB: Anti-IgE, indicated for food allergy age ≥1, reduces severity; NOT a cure - ongoing treatment required for protection
OIT cures food allergy permanently
No treatments exist beyond avoidance
Omalizumab is contraindicated in food allergy

questions.explanation: Emerging food allergy treatments: (1) ORAL IMMUNOTHERAPY (OIT): Daily ingestion of gradually increasing allergen doses under medical supervision. approved Palforzia (peanut) for ages 4-17. Achieves DESENSITIZATION (raised threshold while on treatment), NOT cure. Requires ongoing daily dosing. Risk of allergic reactions during buildup; (2) OMALIZUMAB: Anti-IgE monoclonal antibody, approved 2024 for food allergy ≥1 year. Reduces reaction severity, allows multiple allergens. 'Allergen-agnostic' approach; (3) SUBLINGUAL (SLIT) and EPICUTANEOUS (EPIT) immunotherapy under investigation. Important: These raise threshold but do NOT eliminate allergy - ongoing treatment needed.

whyChain.answer: Palforzia is approved for ages 4-17. Younger children may have higher response rates but also outgrow allergies more often. Shared decision-making: OIT benefits (raised threshold, less anxiety) vs risks (daily dosing, reactions, GI symptoms in ~10%). Some families prefer strict avoidance; others want protection from accidental exposure.

Omalizumab binds free IgE, reducing IgE available to trigger mast cells. It's 'allergen-agnostic' - works for multiple food allergies simultaneously. Doesn't require eating the allergen daily. May be combined with OIT. Approved for children ≥1 year with multiple food allergies.

sources.text: StatPearls: Food Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The family asks if he will outgrow his peanut allergy.
questions.question: What is the natural history of food allergies?
questions.hint: Consider which allergies are more likely to resolve.

options.text: All food allergies are lifelong
No way to predict resolution
Peanut allergy is always outgrown

MILK and EGG: Majority outgrow by school age (80-90% by age 16); PEANUT and TREE NUTS: ~20% outgrow, most persist; FISH and SHELLFISH: Usually persist lifelong; Higher initial sIgE predicts persistence; Resolution can be confirmed by oral food challenge

questions.explanation: Natural history varies by allergen: (1) MILK: 50% resolve by age 5, ~80-90% by age 16; (2) EGG: 50% resolve by age 5, ~80-90% by age 16; many tolerate baked egg before whole egg; (3) PEANUT: Only ~20% outgrow; higher Ara h 2, larger SPT, earlier/severe reactions predict persistence; (4) TREE NUTS: ~10% outgrow, most persist; (5) FISH/SHELLFISH: Usually lifelong. Predictors of persistence: Higher sIgE levels, multiple food allergies, atopic comorbidities (asthma, eczema), severe initial reactions. This child with high peanut sIgE (45) and Ara h 2 (28) is less likely to outgrow.

whyChain.answer: He has moderate egg sIgE (8 kU/L) and tolerates baked egg. This is common - baked egg is less allergenic (heat denatures ovomucoid). He may tolerate baked egg now and likely outgrow whole-egg allergy. Could consider introducing baked egg under guidance, or monitor with periodic testing.

sources.text: StatPearls: Food Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! MILK and EGG: Majority outgrow by school age (80-90% by age 16); PEANUT and TREE NUTS: ~20% outgrow, most persist; FISH and SHELLFISH: Usually persist lifelong; Higher initial sIgE predicts persistence; Resolution can be confirmed by oral food challenge.
correct.deeperWhy: He has moderate egg sIgE (8 kU/L) and tolerates baked egg. This is common - baked egg is less allergenic (heat denatures ovomucoid). He may tolerate baked egg now and likely outgrow whole-egg allergy.
correct.clinicalPearl: Natural history varies by allergen: (1) MILK: 50% resolve by age 5, ~80-90% by age 16; (2) EGG: 50% resolve by age 5, ~80-90% by age 16; many tolerate baked egg before whole egg; (3) PEANUT: Only ~20%

questions.stem: Summarizing the evaluation and management plan for this child.
questions.question: What is the complete assessment and plan for this child?

options.text: Diagnosis: Peanut allergy confirmed (history + high sIgE/Ara h 2); cashew sensitization (avoid pending challenge); egg: tolerates baked, monitor. Management: Strict peanut/cashew avoidance, epinephrine auto-injector x2, written action plan, school notification. Follow-up: Annual monitoring, discuss OIT when age-appropriate, consider tree nut challenge for low-sensitized nuts
No follow-up needed
Avoid all foods with positive testing forever
Antihistamines sufficient for future reactions

questions.explanation: Complete plan: (1) DIAGNOSIS: Peanut allergy confirmed (convincing history + sIgE 45, Ara h 2 28). Cashew sensitization - avoid (high sIgE), consider future challenge. Egg - baked tolerated, may outgrow, monitor. Milk - not allergic; (2) AVOIDANCE: Strict peanut, tree nut (especially cashew/pistachio) avoidance. Read labels, cross-contamination education; (3) EMERGENCY PREP: Epinephrine auto-injector (weight-appropriate), prescribe 2, written action plan, train family and school; (4) FOLLOW-UP: Annual visits, repeat sIgE to monitor trends, consider OIT discussion at age 4+, supervised challenge for low-risk nuts if desired; (5) PREVENTION: Early introduction of other allergens in younger siblings per LEAP study principles.

whyChain.why: What about his eczema and asthma risk?
whyChain.answer: Atopic march: Children with eczema + food allergy are at increased asthma risk (~40-60%). Monitor for respiratory symptoms. Good eczema control may reduce sensitization risk. His eczema should be optimally managed. Family should be counseled about asthma symptoms.

sources.text: StatPearls: Food Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Sampson HA, et al. Food allergy: A practice parameter update—2014. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2014;134(5):1016-1025.e43. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected food allergy?

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in food allergy. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in food allergy. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in food allergy. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

A.misconception: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
A.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
C.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
D.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of food allergy. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

A.misconception: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
A.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

B.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
B.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.misconception: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.
D.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: When managing food allergy, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

A.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
A.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.
C.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.misconception: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.
D.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization.
correct.deeperWhy: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.
correct.clinicalPearl: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose tr
